simple_explanation: To move from one place to another.
daily_life_use: Used when leaving a place or moving towards a destination.
common_mistakes: Saying 'I go to home' instead of 'I go home'.
hindi_confusion_correction: In Hindi, we say 'मैं घर जा रहा हूँ', in English 'I am going home' (no 'to').
formal_informal_note: Used in both formal and informal situations.

simple_explanation: To move toward the speaker or a specific place.
daily_life_use: Used when inviting someone or talking about arriving.
examples: Can you come to my party?
common_mistakes: Using 'come' when you mean 'go' (e.g., 'I am coming there' sounds unnatural if you mean going away).
hindi_confusion_correction: Remember 'come' is movement towards the speaker, 'go' is away.

simple_explanation: To move on foot at a normal speed.
daily_life_use: Going places by foot, or exercising.
common_mistakes: Pronouncing the 'l' in walk. The 'l' is silent.
hindi_confusion_correction: Hindi 'चलना' can mean 'go' or 'walk', but English 'walk' strictly means moving on foot.

simple_explanation: To move on foot faster than walking.
daily_life_use: Exercising or hurrying to catch something.
spoken_use: I have to run to the store.
natural_native_use: I gotta run! (meaning I have to leave quickly)
common_mistakes: Using 'run' for machines in the wrong way (though 'the car is running' is correct).
hindi_confusion_correction: Can also mean managing something (e.g., 'run a business' - बिज़नेस चलाना).

simple_explanation: To rest your body on a chair or the ground.
daily_life_use: Resting, working at a desk, or eating.
examples: She was sitting on the floor.
common_mistakes: Confusing 'seat' (noun) and 'sit' (verb).
hindi_confusion_correction: 'Seat' is the object you sit on, 'Sit' is the action.

simple_explanation: To be on your feet in an upright position.
daily_life_use: Waiting in a line or getting up from a chair.
natural_native_use: I can't stand it (idiom meaning I hate it).
examples: He was standing by the door.
common_mistakes: Saying 'standing on my head' when meaning 'upright'.
hindi_confusion_correction: Also used for tolerating ('मैं इसे सह नहीं सकता' = 'I can't stand it').

simple_explanation: To push yourself off the ground into the air.
daily_life_use: Playing sports or avoiding an obstacle.
examples: The cat jumped onto the table.
Kids love to jump on the bed.
common_mistakes: Using jump when 'hop' or 'skip' is more accurate.
hindi_confusion_correction: Jump implies leaving the ground completely.

simple_explanation: To drop down towards the ground accidentally.
daily_life_use: Tripping over something or dropping items.
common_mistakes: Confusing 'fall' (intransitive) with 'drop' (transitive).
hindi_confusion_correction: गिरना = fall (happens to you), गिराना = drop (you do it to an object).

simple_explanation: To come to an end or cause to not continue.
daily_life_use: Stopping a car, ending an activity.
natural_native_use: Can we stop by the store? (visit quickly)
examples: The bus stopped at the station.
common_mistakes: Using 'stop to talk' vs 'stop talking' (they mean different things).
hindi_confusion_correction: Stop talking (बात करना बंद करो), Stop to talk (बात करने के लिए रुको).

simple_explanation: To stay in a place until an expected event happens.
daily_life_use: Waiting for a bus or a friend.
examples: I will wait for you outside.
She waited an hour for the train.
common_mistakes: Saying 'wait me' instead of 'wait for me'.
hindi_confusion_correction: Always use 'for' after wait when mentioning the object (Wait for me).

simple_explanation: To put food in your mouth and swallow it.
daily_life_use: Having meals like breakfast, lunch, or dinner.
examples: I eat an apple every day.
What did you eat for lunch?
common_mistakes: Saying 'I am eating water' instead of 'drinking water'.
hindi_confusion_correction: In English, 'eat' is only for solids; 'drink' or 'take' is for liquids and medicines.

simple_explanation: To take a liquid into your mouth and swallow it.
daily_life_use: Consuming water, tea, coffee, or juice.
spoken_use: I need a drink of water.
natural_native_use: Let's go grab a drink. (Often implies alcohol in native contexts)
examples: Make sure to drink plenty of water.
common_mistakes: Using 'drink' for soup. We say 'eat soup' or 'have soup'.
hindi_confusion_correction: 'Drink' without an object often implies drinking alcohol in Western culture.

simple_explanation: To rest with your eyes closed and your mind inactive.
daily_life_use: Resting at night.
natural_native_use: I'm going to crash. / Hit the sack.
examples: I sleep for 8 hours every night.
common_mistakes: Saying 'I was sleep' instead of 'I was sleeping' or 'I was asleep'.
hindi_confusion_correction: Asleep is an adjective (I am asleep), sleep is the verb.

simple_explanation: To stop sleeping.
daily_life_use: Starting your day in the morning.
examples: Please wake me up early tomorrow.
common_mistakes: Saying 'awake me' instead of 'wake me up'.
hindi_confusion_correction: Wake up implies stopping sleep. Get up implies physically leaving the bed.

simple_explanation: To prepare food by heating it.
daily_life_use: Making meals in the kitchen.
common_mistakes: Calling a person who cooks a 'cooker'. A cooker is the machine; the person is a 'cook'.
hindi_confusion_correction: Don't translate 'खाना बनाने वाला' to 'cooker'. Use 'cook' or 'chef'.

simple_explanation: To remove dirt or marks from something.
daily_life_use: Tidying up the house or washing items.
examples: I need to clean my room.
common_mistakes: Confusing 'clean' (action/state) with 'clear' (empty of obstacles).
hindi_confusion_correction: Use clean for dirt, clear for clutter or obstacles.

simple_explanation: To clean with water and usually soap.
daily_life_use: Cleaning clothes, dishes, or hands.
natural_native_use: Do the washing up. (British for washing dishes)
examples: Please wash your hands before eating.
He washed his car on Sunday.
common_mistakes: Saying 'wash the house' instead of 'clean the house'. Wash strictly implies using water.
hindi_confusion_correction: Only use 'wash' when water is heavily involved.

simple_explanation: To clean dirt from the floor using a broom.
daily_life_use: Daily house cleaning chore.
natural_native_use: Give the floor a quick sweep.
examples: She swept the floor this morning.
He is sweeping the dust away.
common_mistakes: Confusing 'sweep' with 'mop' (which uses water).
hindi_confusion_correction: झाड़ू लगाना is sweep, पोछा लगाना is mop.

simple_explanation: To clean or dry a surface by rubbing it with a cloth.
daily_life_use: Cleaning tables, counters, or drying hands.
examples: Wipe your feet before coming in.
She wiped the tears from her face.
common_mistakes: Using 'clean' instead of 'wipe' when specifically referring to using a cloth on a small spill.
hindi_confusion_correction: Wipe specifically focuses on the rubbing action to remove liquid or dust.

simple_explanation: To use force to move something away from you.
daily_life_use: Opening doors, moving heavy objects.
common_mistakes: Pushing a door that says 'pull'.
hindi_confusion_correction: Push = away from you. Pull = towards you.

simple_explanation: To use force to move something toward you.
daily_life_use: Opening drawers, dragging objects.
examples: Pull the door to open it.
She pulled him out of the water.
common_mistakes: Confusing push and pull on doors.
hindi_confusion_correction: Pull is always motion towards your own body.

simple_explanation: To move something upward.
daily_life_use: Picking up bags, boxes, or children.
common_mistakes: Saying 'pick up' when it involves serious heavy lifting.
hindi_confusion_correction: Pick up is casual (like a pencil), lift involves more effort or weight.

simple_explanation: To let something fall accidentally or intentionally.
daily_life_use: Losing grip on an object.
examples: Please drop me at the station. (meaning drop off)
common_mistakes: Saying 'I fell the glass' instead of 'I dropped the glass'.
hindi_confusion_correction: Things fall (गिरना), but people drop things (गिराना).

simple_explanation: To have something in your hands or arms.
daily_life_use: Carrying bags, holding hands.
common_mistakes: Using 'catch' when 'hold' is meant. You catch a moving ball, you hold a still cup.
hindi_confusion_correction: In Hindi, 'पकड़ना' is used for both catch and hold. In English, they are strictly different.

simple_explanation: To grab and hold something that is moving through the air.
daily_life_use: Playing sports, taking a bus or train.
natural_native_use: Catch a bus. / Catch a cold.
examples: I need to hurry to catch the train.
common_mistakes: Using 'catch' for stationary objects.
hindi_confusion_correction: Only use catch for things in motion (a ball, a train) or illnesses.

simple_explanation: To send something through the air with force using your arm.
daily_life_use: Disposing of trash, playing catch.
examples: Throw the garbage in the bin.
He threw the ball to his friend.
common_mistakes: Confusing throw and drop. Drop is just letting go, throw involves force.
hindi_confusion_correction: फेंकना = throw. छोड़ना = let go / drop.

simple_explanation: To hold something and take it from one place to another.
daily_life_use: Carrying bags from grocery store, carrying a baby.
examples: He is carrying a heavy suitcase.
Let me carry that for you.
common_mistakes: Using 'take' when 'carry' is more specific about physical burden.
hindi_confusion_correction: Carry emphasizes the physical effort of holding while moving.

simple_explanation: To operate and control the direction of a car or other vehicle.
daily_life_use: Commuting to work, taking a road trip.
examples: She drives to work every day.
Can you drive a manual car?
common_mistakes: Saying 'drive a bicycle'. You 'ride' a bicycle or a bike.
hindi_confusion_correction: In Hindi 'चलाना' applies to bikes and cars. In English: Drive a car, Ride a bike.

simple_explanation: To sit on and control a bicycle, motorcycle, or animal.
daily_life_use: Riding a bike for exercise or commuting.
examples: He likes to ride his motorcycle.
She rode a horse on vacation.
common_mistakes: Saying 'drive a bike' instead of 'ride a bike'.
hindi_confusion_correction: Ride is for things you sit ON top of (bike, horse). Drive is for things you sit INSIDE (car).

simple_explanation: To say words in order to exchange information or ideas with someone.
daily_life_use: Having a conversation with a friend or colleague.
examples: She was talking on the phone.
common_mistakes: Saying 'discuss about' instead of 'talk about'. Discuss takes no preposition.
hindi_confusion_correction: Talk is conversational and usually implies two-way communication.

simple_explanation: To use your voice to say words, often used for languages or formal situations.
daily_life_use: Giving a presentation or mentioning what language you use.
natural_native_use: Can I speak to John? (on the phone)
examples: The manager will speak to the team.
common_mistakes: Saying 'say English' instead of 'speak English'.
hindi_confusion_correction: Speak is used for languages and one-way formal communication. Talk is for informal conversation.

simple_explanation: To pay attention to a sound or to what someone is saying.
daily_life_use: Enjoying music or paying attention in class.
natural_native_use: Hear me out. / Have a listen.
examples: I love to listen to music.
She wasn't listening to the teacher.
common_mistakes: Saying 'listen music' instead of 'listen to music'. Always use 'to'.
hindi_confusion_correction: Listen is an active choice. Hear is passive (happens automatically).

simple_explanation: To become aware of sound through your ears without actively trying.
daily_life_use: Noticing a loud noise outside.
natural_native_use: I can't hear you over the noise.
common_mistakes: Saying 'I am hearing you' instead of 'I hear you' (it's a non-continuous verb usually).
hindi_confusion_correction: In Hindi 'सुनना' is used for both listen and hear. English separates them by intent.

simple_explanation: To direct your eyes in order to see.
daily_life_use: Searching for items or paying attention to visuals.
examples: I am looking for my keys.
common_mistakes: Saying 'look the picture' instead of 'look at the picture'.
hindi_confusion_correction: Look is intentional (like listen). See is automatic (like hear).

simple_explanation: To notice or become aware of someone or something by using your eyes.
daily_life_use: Noticing things around you.
natural_native_use: I see what you mean. (Meaning I understand)
examples: I can see the mountains from here.
common_mistakes: Using 'see' when talking about watching a movie at home (use watch instead).
hindi_confusion_correction: See is passive. You see things just because your eyes are open.

simple_explanation: To look at something for a period of time, especially something that is moving or changing.
daily_life_use: Viewing TV, movies, or sports.
examples: He is watching a football game.
common_mistakes: Saying 'see TV' instead of 'watch TV'.
hindi_confusion_correction: Watch is used for moving pictures (TV, sports, kids playing). Look is for still objects (a photo).

simple_explanation: To look at words or symbols and understand what they mean.
daily_life_use: Reading books, messages, or news.
spoken_use: I read an interesting article today.
common_mistakes: Mispronouncing the past tense. Present is 'read' (reed), past is 'read' (red).
hindi_confusion_correction: Study (अध्ययन करना) means to learn deeply. Read (पढ़ना) just means to look at words.

simple_explanation: To make marks that represent letters, words, or numbers on a surface.
daily_life_use: Taking notes, sending emails, or doing homework.
natural_native_use: Jot it down. / Drop me a line.
examples: She writes poetry in her free time.
common_mistakes: Confusing the spelling of 'write' and 'right'.
hindi_confusion_correction: No major confusion, straightforward translation.

simple_explanation: To move something so that a space is no longer covered or blocked.
daily_life_use: Opening doors, windows, books, or boxes.
examples: He opened his book to page 10.
common_mistakes: Saying 'open the light' instead of 'turn on the light'.
hindi_confusion_correction: In Hindi, 'खोलना' is sometimes used for switching on electronics (टीवी खोल दो), but in English, electronics are 'turned on'.

simple_explanation: To shut something that is open.
daily_life_use: Closing doors, shutting laptops, finishing business hours.
examples: Please close the door behind you.
common_mistakes: Saying 'close the light' instead of 'turn off the light'.
hindi_confusion_correction: Just like 'open', don't use 'close' for electronics. Use 'turn off' or 'switch off'.

simple_explanation: To take air into the lungs and let it out.
daily_life_use: Used regarding health, relaxation, or exercise.
natural_native_use: I can't breathe in this smoke.
examples: The doctor told me to breathe deeply.
common_mistakes: Confusing 'breathe' (verb) with 'breath' (noun).
hindi_confusion_correction: सांस लेना = Breathe (Verb). सांस = Breath (Noun).

simple_explanation: Used to politely get someone's attention or ask them to move.
daily_life_use: Getting someone's attention in public or passing by someone.
spoken_use: Excuse me, where is the station?
examples: He made an excuse for being late.
common_mistakes: Using 'sorry' to get attention instead of 'excuse me'.
hindi_confusion_correction: Use 'excuse me' before you bother someone, use 'sorry' after you bother them.
formal_informal_note: Polite and appropriate for all settings.

simple_explanation: To utter words so as to convey information.
daily_life_use: Reporting what someone else spoke.
common_mistakes: Saying 'say him' instead of 'say to him' or 'tell him'.
hindi_confusion_correction: Say focuses on the words spoken. Tell focuses on the person receiving the information.

simple_explanation: To communicate information, facts, or news to someone.
daily_life_use: Giving instructions or sharing a story.
common_mistakes: Saying 'tell to me' instead of 'tell me'.
hindi_confusion_correction: Never use 'to' after tell when stating the listener. 'Tell me' (मुझे बताओ), not 'Tell to me'.

simple_explanation: To say something in order to obtain an answer or some information.
daily_life_use: Requesting help or posing a question.
spoken_use: Can I ask you a question?
examples: She asked me for some money.
If you don't know, just ask.
common_mistakes: Saying 'ask to him' instead of 'ask him'.
hindi_confusion_correction: 'Ask for' means मांगना (ask for water). 'Ask' means पूछना (ask a question).

simple_explanation: To be aware of through observation, inquiry, or information.
daily_life_use: Expressing familiarity with facts or people.
common_mistakes: Pronouncing the 'k'. It is silent.
hindi_confusion_correction: 'Get to know' means किसी को अच्छे से जानना.

simple_explanation: To have a particular opinion, belief, or idea about someone or something.
daily_life_use: Giving an opinion or considering a problem.
examples: What do you think of this shirt?
I am thinking about moving to London.
common_mistakes: Saying 'I am thinking that' when expressing a firm opinion. Usually it's 'I think that'.
hindi_confusion_correction: In Hindi 'मुझे लगता है' is translated to 'I think', not 'I feel'.

simple_explanation: To perceive the intended meaning of words, a language, or a speaker.
daily_life_use: Confirming that you grasped a concept or instruction.
spoken_use: I don't understand this math problem.
examples: Do you understand what I'm saying?
common_mistakes: Saying 'I am not understanding'. It's usually 'I don't understand' (stative verb).
hindi_confusion_correction: Translation is exact. समझना = understand.

simple_explanation: To have in or be able to bring to one's mind an awareness of someone or something from the past.
daily_life_use: Recalling a memory or reminding yourself to do something.
examples: I remember playing here as a kid.
common_mistakes: Confusing 'remember' (internal memory) with 'remind' (making someone else remember).
hindi_confusion_correction: याद रखना = remember. याद दिलाना = remind.

simple_explanation: To fail to remember.
daily_life_use: Admitting you didn't remember to do a task.
examples: Don't forget to call your mother.
common_mistakes: Saying 'I forgot my book at home'. The better phrase is 'I left my book at home'.
hindi_confusion_correction: You forget facts, you leave objects.

simple_explanation: To accept that something is true, especially without proof.
daily_life_use: Expressing faith, trust, or an opinion.
common_mistakes: Saying 'believe on' instead of 'believe in'.
hindi_confusion_correction: Used for both trust (मुझे तुम पर विश्वास है) and opinion (मेरा मानना है कि...).

simple_explanation: Used to politely get someone's attention.
daily_life_use: When interrupting or trying to pass by.
examples: Excuse me, where is the bus stop?
common_mistakes: Using it aggressively when trying to pass.
hindi_confusion_correction: Hindi 'suniye' translates to 'excuse me' when getting attention.

simple_explanation: To express gratitude.
daily_life_use: When someone helps you.
common_mistakes: Saying 'thank' without 'you' (unless saying 'thanks').
hindi_confusion_correction: In English, thanking is much more common than in Hindi for small things.
formal_informal_note: Thanks is informal; Thank you is standard/formal.

simple_explanation: To move or travel to a place.
daily_life_use: Giving instructions or talking about travel.
common_mistakes: Saying 'go to home' instead of 'go home'.
hindi_confusion_correction: Hindi 'jaana' is go.

simple_explanation: To move towards the speaker.
daily_life_use: Inviting someone.
common_mistakes: Using 'come' when you mean 'go' (e.g. 'I am coming to my house' instead of 'going').
hindi_confusion_correction: Hindi 'aana' is come.

simple_explanation: To direct your eyes towards something.
daily_life_use: Asking someone to pay visual attention.
examples: I am looking for my keys.
common_mistakes: Confusing 'look', 'see', and 'watch'. Look is intentional.
hindi_confusion_correction: Hindi 'dekhna' covers look, see, and watch. English separates them.

simple_explanation: To perceive with the eyes.
daily_life_use: Noticing things naturally.
examples: I can see the mountains from here.
common_mistakes: Using 'see' in the continuous tense incorrectly ('I am seeing a bird' -> 'I see a bird').
hindi_confusion_correction: See is automatic, you don't try to do it.

simple_explanation: To perceive sound.
daily_life_use: Noticing noises.
examples: Speak up, I can't hear you.
common_mistakes: Saying 'I am hearing you'. Use 'I hear you'.
hindi_confusion_correction: Hear is automatic, listen is intentional.

simple_explanation: To pay attention to sound.
daily_life_use: Focusing on what someone is saying.
natural_native_use: I am listening to a song.
examples: She likes to listen to music.
common_mistakes: Forgetting the preposition 'to' (e.g., 'Listen me' -> 'Listen to me').
hindi_confusion_correction: Hindi 'sunna' is used for both hear and listen.

simple_explanation: To speak words.
daily_life_use: Quoting or stating something.
examples: She said that she is happy.
common_mistakes: Confusing 'say' and 'tell'. Say is used without a direct object often.
hindi_confusion_correction: Say is focusing on the words spoken.

simple_explanation: To communicate information to someone.
daily_life_use: Giving information or instructions.
common_mistakes: Saying 'tell to me'. It should just be 'tell me'.
hindi_confusion_correction: Tell must be followed by a person (who you are telling).

simple_explanation: To speak in order to give information or express ideas.
daily_life_use: Having a conversation.
spoken_use: I need to talk to you.
common_mistakes: Saying 'we talked to each other' is fine, but 'discuss about' is wrong.
hindi_confusion_correction: Talk is generally informal or for two-way conversation.

simple_explanation: To talk, or use a language.
daily_life_use: Formal conversation or languages.
examples: I need to speak with the manager.
common_mistakes: Confusing 'talk' and 'speak'. Speak is more formal and used for languages.
hindi_confusion_correction: Speak is a one-way communication or language ability.
formal_informal_note: Speak is formal; talk is informal.

simple_explanation: To put food in the mouth and chew it.
daily_life_use: Talking about having a meal.
examples: What do you want to eat?
common_mistakes: Saying 'eat water' or 'eat medicine' (use drink and take).
hindi_confusion_correction: Hindi 'khaana' is used for many things, English 'eat' is strict for food.

simple_explanation: To take liquid into the mouth and swallow.
daily_life_use: Talking about liquids.
common_mistakes: Using 'drink' as a noun can sometimes imply drinking alcohol.
hindi_confusion_correction: In Hindi 'peena' can mean smoking too, English 'drink' does not.

simple_explanation: To stop sleeping.
daily_life_use: Morning routines.
examples: I wake up at 6 am every day.
common_mistakes: Confusing 'wake up' (open eyes) with 'get up' (leave the bed).
hindi_confusion_correction: Hindi 'uthna' is used for both wake up and get up.

simple_explanation: To move very fast on foot.
daily_life_use: Exercise or hurrying.
examples: The dog is running after the ball.
common_mistakes: Using 'run' for vehicles ('the car is running' means engine is on, not moving).
hindi_confusion_correction: Hindi 'daudna' is run.

simple_explanation: To rest weight on the buttocks.
daily_life_use: Asking someone to take a seat.
examples: We were sitting on the floor.
common_mistakes: Saying 'sit your seat' (use 'take your seat').
hindi_confusion_correction: Hindi 'baithna' is sit.

simple_explanation: To be in an upright position on the feet.
daily_life_use: When not sitting.
common_mistakes: Confusing 'stand' with 'stay'.
hindi_confusion_correction: Hindi 'khade hona' is stand.

simple_explanation: To hand over or provide.
daily_life_use: Transferring items.
common_mistakes: Saying 'give a test' for students (students 'take a test', teachers 'give a test').
hindi_confusion_correction: Hindi 'dena' translates to give.

simple_explanation: To get into one's possession.
daily_life_use: Receiving or removing items.
common_mistakes: Confusing take and bring. Take is away from the speaker, bring is towards.
hindi_confusion_correction: Hindi 'lena' translates to take.

simple_explanation: To obtain, receive, or understand.
daily_life_use: Very common verb for many actions.
spoken_use: I need to get a new phone.
examples: I need to get some sleep.
common_mistakes: Overusing 'get' instead of more specific verbs.
hindi_confusion_correction: Hindi 'milna' or 'samajhna' matches 'get'.
formal_informal_note: Get is very common in informal English.

simple_explanation: To move something into a specific place.
daily_life_use: Placing objects.
natural_native_use: Where did you put my keys?
examples: He put the book in the bag.
common_mistakes: Saying 'keep' instead of 'put' (keep means retain, put means place).
hindi_confusion_correction: Hindi 'rakhna' is both keep and put. In English, they are different.

simple_explanation: To perform an action.
daily_life_use: General activities or tasks.
common_mistakes: Confusing 'do' and 'make'. Do is for general activities, make is for creating.
hindi_confusion_correction: Hindi 'karna' is do.

simple_explanation: To create or construct something.
daily_life_use: Creating food, noise, messes.
common_mistakes: Confusing 'do a mistake' with 'make a mistake'.
hindi_confusion_correction: Hindi 'banana' translates to make.

simple_explanation: To possess or own.
daily_life_use: Talking about belongings or eating/drinking.
common_mistakes: Saying 'I am having a car' (incorrect, 'have' for possession is not continuous).
hindi_confusion_correction: Hindi 'mere paas hai' is 'I have'.

simple_explanation: To be aware of through observation or information.
daily_life_use: Stating facts or recognizing someone.
common_mistakes: Pronouncing the 'k' (it is silent).
hindi_confusion_correction: Hindi 'jaanna' or 'pata hona' is know.

simple_explanation: To have a particular opinion or belief.
daily_life_use: Sharing opinions.
examples: I am thinking about my future.
common_mistakes: Saying 'I am thinking that...' instead of 'I think that...'.
hindi_confusion_correction: Hindi 'sochna' is think.

simple_explanation: To have a desire for something.
daily_life_use: Expressing desires.
common_mistakes: Saying 'I want to eating' instead of 'I want to eat'.
hindi_confusion_correction: Hindi 'chaahna' is want.

simple_explanation: Look at and comprehend the meaning of written matter.
daily_life_use: Books, signs, messages.
common_mistakes: Confusing present 'read' (reed) and past 'read' (red).
hindi_confusion_correction: Hindi 'padhna' translates to both read and study.

simple_explanation: Mark letters, words, or symbols on a surface.
daily_life_use: Putting thoughts on paper.
common_mistakes: Pronouncing the 'w' (it is silent).
hindi_confusion_correction: Hindi 'likhna' is write.

simple_explanation: To get something by paying money.
daily_life_use: Shopping.
spoken_use: I want to buy a car.
examples: He wants to buy a house.
common_mistakes: Confusing 'buy' and 'bring'.
hindi_confusion_correction: Hindi 'khareedna' is buy.

simple_explanation: Give or hand over in exchange for money.
daily_life_use: Business or disposing of items.
spoken_use: I want to sell my old phone.
common_mistakes: Confusing noun 'sale' and verb 'sell'.
hindi_confusion_correction: Hindi 'bechna' is sell.

simple_explanation: To come together with someone at the same place.
daily_life_use: Used when making plans with friends or being introduced to someone new.
natural_native_use: Let's meet up for coffee later.
examples: We are meeting at the station.
common_mistakes: Saying 'I met with my friend'. Just say 'I met my friend'. ('Meet with' is for formal meetings).
hindi_confusion_correction: In Hindi we say 'मैं राम से मिला' (with/to), but in English it's direct: 'I met Ram'.

simple_explanation: To speak in order to give information or express ideas.
daily_life_use: Used for conversations, gossiping, or discussions.
natural_native_use: I was just talking to him.
common_mistakes: Saying 'He is talking with me' instead of 'He is talking to me' (both are fine, but 'to' is more common).
hindi_confusion_correction: The 'L' in talk is completely silent. It's 'tawk', not 'tall-k'.

simple_explanation: To express disagreement in a loud or angry way.
daily_life_use: Used when couples or friends have a fight with words.
natural_native_use: They are always arguing over money.
examples: The kids are arguing about the game.
I don't want to argue anymore.
common_mistakes: Confusing 'argue' with 'fight'. Arguing is with words, fighting is usually physical.
hindi_confusion_correction: Perfect translation for 'बहस करना'.

simple_explanation: To have the same opinion about something.
daily_life_use: Used to say 'yes, you are right' in conversations.
natural_native_use: We finally agreed on a price.
common_mistakes: Saying 'I am agree'. The correct phrase is 'I agree'. 'Agree' is an action verb, not an adjective.
hindi_confusion_correction: Hindi speakers often use 'am' because of 'मैं सहमत हूँ' (am), but in English, 'agree' is the main verb.

simple_explanation: To say sorry for something that you have done wrong.
daily_life_use: Used when you make a mistake and want to fix the relationship.
examples: I want to apologize for what I said.
common_mistakes: Saying 'I apologize you'. You must say 'I apologize TO you'.
hindi_confusion_correction: More formal than just saying 'Sorry'.
formal_informal_note: A polite, slightly formal way to say sorry.

simple_explanation: To stop feeling angry or resentful toward someone for an offense.
daily_life_use: Used after an apology to mend a relationship.
natural_native_use: I can't forgive him for what he did.
common_mistakes: Using 'forgave' (past tense) when you should use 'forgiven' (past participle) - 'I have forgiven him'.
hindi_confusion_correction: Opposite of 'punish'.

simple_explanation: To refuse to take notice of or acknowledge someone.
daily_life_use: Used when you deliberately don't look at or listen to someone.
common_mistakes: Pronouncing it as 'ig-nore-ance'. The 'ance' makes it a noun (ignorance = à¤…à¤œà¥à¤žà¤¾à¤¨à¤¤à¤¾), 'ignore' is the verb.
hindi_confusion_correction: Perfect translation for 'अनदेखा करना' or 'नज़रअंदाज़ करना'.
